title: Integrated pest management: a working SOP
sub: A plain-language standard operating procedure for keeping pests and disease out of an indoor cannabis grow: how to scout, when to act, how to use good bugs and sprays, and how to keep records that hold up.

sections.title: What IPM is and why a grow lives or dies by it
sections.summary: Integrated Pest Management (IPM) is the routine of catching pests and diseases before they spread, using the lightest control that works and escalating only when you have to. It is not one spray or one bug release; it is a loop of evaluate, decide, control, repeat, run across every room from mothers and clones through veg and flower. The stakes are total: a single missed mite colony or a powdery-mildew outbreak can destroy an entire harvest, and in a regulated facility a pesticide misstep can fail residue testing and bin the whole batch. This guide assumes you know nothing and defines every term as it appears.
points: IPM = a repeating loop (evaluate, decide, control, evaluate), not a one-off treatment
Lightest effective control first; escalate to sprays only when thresholds force it
Applies to every stage: mothers, clones, veg, and flower
A small problem caught early is cheap; a missed one can cost the whole crop

figures.name: ipm_loop
figures.desc: Circular flow: Scout, then Identify, then check Threshold, then Decide control, then Act, then Record, looping back to Scout

claims_needing_cite: A single missed pest colony or mildew outbreak can destroy an entire harvest
Pesticide residue failures can void a whole regulated batch

sections.title: Every term you need, defined once
sections.summary: Before the procedure, here is the language. Scouting is the deliberate visual inspection of plants for pests, damage, or disease. An action threshold is the pest level that triggers a specific response. Biological control (biocontrol) means releasing predatory insects or mites (the good bugs) to eat the pests. A foliar spray is a liquid applied to leaves and stems; a drench is applied to the root zone. REI (re-entry interval, also called REI) is how long after a spray before anyone may safely enter the room. A withholding period is how long after treatment before the crop can be harvested for sale.
points: Scouting: planned visual inspection for early signs of trouble
Action threshold: the pest level that triggers a defined response
Biocontrol / good bugs: predator insects and mites that eat pests
Foliar vs drench: leaf-and-stem spray vs root-zone application
REI: wait time before re-entering a sprayed room; withholding period: wait time before harvest

figures.name: term_map
figures.desc: Two-column glossary table: term and one-line plain-English definition for scouting, threshold, biocontrol, foliar, drench, REI, withholding period, quarantine, frass, sachet

sections.title: Scouting: find it before it finds you
sections.summary: Scouting is the engine of IPM; everything downstream depends on catching pests early. A practical baseline is for the room lead to visually inspect at least 5 percent of the room at every check, logging any finding immediately, while dedicated IPM scouts do deeper precision inspections. Work a fixed route every time: scout the tables and substrate for frass (insect droppings) and debris, then the trunk and stems, then the undersides of leaves (where mites and thrips hide), then leaf tops. Yellow sticky traps at the base of the stem and at canopy height give an early-warning catch of stray fungus gnats, aphids, and thrips; replace one trap weekly, write the date on it, and use a jeweler's loupe to confirm tiny pests like russet mites that are invisible to the naked eye.
points: Inspect at least 5% of the room every check; deeper scouting by trained scouts
Fixed route: substrate, then trunk/stems, then leaf undersides, then leaf tops
Yellow sticky traps at stem base and canopy; replace one weekly and date it
Carry a loupe: russet mites and broad mites need magnification to confirm

figures.name: scout_route
figures.desc: Side view of a plant with numbered inspection stops: 1 substrate/tray, 2 base sticky trap, 3 trunk and stems, 4 leaf undersides, 5 leaf tops, 6 canopy sticky trap

figures.name: sticky_trend
figures.desc: Weekly insect counts on dated sticky traps trending up vs flat, illustrating early-warning detection

claims_needing_cite: A 5% per-check inspection sample is an effective baseline for room scouting

sections.title: Action thresholds: knowing exactly when to act
sections.summary: A threshold turns observation into a decision so nobody has to guess. A workable two-tier model: at the first sign of a single mite, aphid, or similar pest, assume there are more, scout the entire room, begin exclusion measures (limit who enters), and line up biological controls; once more than 3 percent of a room is affected, escalate to approved pesticide applications, subject to the plant's growth stage and management sign-off. Growth stage is itself a hard gate: in a regulated soilless facility, plants are treated only from mothering through day 21 of flower, with no foliar treatment after buds form (a light antimicrobial rinse may be the only exception). For a serious infestation that appears after day 21 of flower, the correct action is to pull and destroy the affected plants rather than spray.
points: Tier 1 (single pest seen): assume more, scout the whole room, start exclusion + biocontrol
Tier 2 (>3% of room affected): escalate to approved pesticide with sign-off
Treatment window: mothering through day 21 of flower only
After day 21 with a serious infestation: pull and destroy, do not spray

figures.name: threshold_ladder
figures.desc: Decision ladder: 0 pests = monitor; 1 pest seen = scout room + exclude + order biocontrol; >3% affected = add approved pesticide; post-day-21 serious = pull and destroy

figures.name: treatment_window
figures.desc: Timeline bar across mothering, clone, veg, flower days 1-21 (treatable, green) and flower day 22 to harvest (no foliar, red)

claims_needing_cite: The single-pest and >3%-of-room thresholds reflect a common commercial action model
Sulphur should be restricted to mothers and vegetative growth, not flowering

sections.title: Biological controls: putting good bugs to work
sections.summary: Biocontrol releases predators that hunt your pests, and they are harmless to the cannabis plant at every stage; if they run out of prey they simply die off and fall away. Match the predator to the pest and the conditions. Amblyseius swirskii is a generalist that eats thrips larvae, whitefly, and broad mites and works best at 77 to 86 degrees Fahrenheit with humidity above 70 percent. Phytoseiulus persimilis is a spider-mite specialist that eats up to 5 adults or 20 eggs a day but needs cooler, humid conditions (ideal 59 to 77 F); Neoseiulus californicus is the tougher spider-mite option that holds up in hotter, drier rooms (60 to 90 F). Stratiolaelaps scimitus is a soil-dwelling mite for fungus gnat larvae and thrips pupae, best deployed when pest pressure is still low. Use hanging release boxes or sachets so predators and their carrier media stay out of the irrigation system.
points: Good bugs are harmless to the plant and self-limit when prey runs out
Generalist (swirskii) for thrips/whitefly/broad mites; warm and humid rooms
Spider mites: persimilis (cool, fast) or californicus (hot/dry, hardy)
Soil pests: stratiolaelaps for fungus gnat larvae and thrips pupae
Deploy via hanging sachets/boxes to keep media out of irrigation

figures.name: predator_match
figures.desc: Predator-to-pest table: species, target pest, ideal temperature range, ideal humidity, generalist vs specialist

figures.name: temp_windows
figures.desc: Horizontal temperature bars (50-95 F) showing each predator's active range overlaid for quick selection

claims_needing_cite: Amblyseius swirskii optimum is 77-86 F and humidity above 70%
Phytoseiulus persimilis eats up to 5 adult mites or 20 eggs per day
Neoseiulus californicus stays active across 60-90 F
Stratiolaelaps scimitus targets fungus gnat larvae and thrips pupae and is best deployed at low pest pressure

sections.title: Spray rotation and safe application
sections.summary: When thresholds force a spray, the rules are about residue safety and effective coverage, not improvisation. Use only products approved for cannabis in your jurisdiction, always follow the label dilution and withholding periods, and never freelance with off-list nutrients or supplements that could fail residue testing. A preventative program can be as simple as a weekly foliar of an approved wash plus sulphur (1 to 3 tablespoons per litre, ideally 3, no more than once every 2 weeks), stepping up to 3 times a week curatively at low pest levels. Rotate chemistries and tank-mix only combinations the labels allow, adding products in the label-specified order; spray when the media is fully saturated (for example the P1-to-P2 irrigation transition) so the product is not pulled into the leaves and burns them. Coverage is everything: hit the top of the media, up the stalk, leaf undersides, then leaf tops, using a fan tip and a dedicated sprayer per product to avoid cross-contamination.
points: Approved products only; follow label dilution and withholding periods exactly
Preventative baseline: weekly approved wash + sulphur; up to 3x/week curative
Sulphur dose 1-3 tbsp/L (ideally 3), max once per 2 weeks, veg/mothers only
Spray on saturated media to prevent leaf uptake and burn
Coverage order: media top, stalk, leaf undersides, leaf tops; fan tip, one sprayer per product

figures.name: spray_rotation
figures.desc: Example weekly rotation grid: day, product/tank-mix, target, REI, withholding period (genericized, no brand recommendations)

figures.name: coverage_order
figures.desc: Plant diagram with arrows numbering spray sequence 1 media top, 2 up the stalk, 3 leaf undersides, 4 leaf tops

claims_needing_cite: Sulphur foliar at 1-3 tbsp/L, max once per 2 weeks, restricted to veg and mothers
Spraying at full media saturation reduces foliar uptake and burn risk
A two-product approach (a citric-acid wash plus sulphur) covers most common pests

sections.title: Sanitation and quarantine: stop pests at the door
sections.summary: Most pests are walked in, so the cheapest control is keeping them out. The biggest single entry point is footwear, so replace shoes or use overboots before entering grow rooms, wash hands on entry and after every break, and wear nitrile gloves at all times, changing them after breaks or after handling chemicals (when in doubt, change them). Every incoming plant goes through quarantine: dunk or wash it in an approved foliar solution on receipt, then hold it in a separate quarantine tent for 2 or more weeks under observation before it joins the crop. Treat all plant waste as hazardous: bag it in the room, weigh and log it, and move it straight to green waste. Keep equipment (scissors, buckets, sprayers, PPE) room-specific and cleaned after every task so a tool never carries pests between rooms.
points: Footwear is the #1 entry route: overboots/shoe change before every room
Gloves on always, change after breaks/chemicals; wash hands on entry
Quarantine new plants: approved wash on receipt + 2+ weeks isolated observation
Plant waste is hazardous: bag in-room, weigh, log, move straight to green waste
Room-specific, cleaned equipment and PPE; never shared between rooms

figures.name: entry_protocol
figures.desc: Entry sequence: change shoes/overboots, wash hands, glove up, foot bath, enter; reverse on exit with glove change

figures.name: quarantine_timeline
figures.desc: New-plant journey: receipt and wash at day 0, quarantine observation days 0-14+, release to crop only if clean

claims_needing_cite: Footwear is the primary way pests are walked into a facility
New plants should be quarantined 2+ weeks under observation before joining the crop

sections.title: The daily routine, the decision flow, and record-keeping
sections.summary: Put it together as a repeatable shift routine. Each day: gear up and enter through the foot bath, walk your scout route, check and date sticky traps, and log every finding (photos and pest IDs uploaded weekly is a good cadence). Run each finding through the decision flow: identify the pest or disease, compare to the threshold, then choose monitor, biocontrol, spray, or pull-and-destroy based on stage and severity. Record everything a regulator or auditor would ask for: product, dilution, area, applicator, REI posted on the door, and the calendar mark outside the room, plus a manager-signed pesticide log. Good records also double as a feedback loop, comparing sticky-trap counts before and after a treatment tells you whether it actually worked.
points: Daily: enter clean, walk the route, check/date traps, log findings (weekly photo upload)
Decision flow: identify, compare to threshold, choose monitor/biocontrol/spray/destroy
Log every spray: product, dilution, area, applicator, REI, manager-signed log
Post REI signage on the door and mark the room calendar during/after application
Use before-and-after trap counts to verify a treatment worked

figures.name: ipm_decision_flow
figures.desc: Master decision flowchart from a finding: Identify -> below threshold? monitor; at tier 1? scout+exclude+biocontrol; over 3%? approved spray (if in window) else pull-and-destroy; then record and re-scout

figures.name: spray_log
figures.desc: Blank pesticide log template: date, product, active ingredient, dilution, area/room, applicator, REI, withholding, manager signature

claims_needing_cite: Every pesticide application must be logged and the log signed by management
REI signage must be posted on the room door and removed only after the interval

sections.title: Common mistakes and what good IPM actually looks like
sections.summary: The frequent failures are predictable: skipping scouting until damage is visible, spraying without confirming the pest, ignoring the treatment window and spraying in late flower, sharing tools or PPE between rooms, and letting good bugs wash into the irrigation system. Avoid airborne fumigators entirely in a flower facility, they can drift into bud and compromise the crop, and check sprayers for metallic hose clamps that can rust and fail heavy-metals testing. Set realistic expectations: IPM does not mean zero pests ever; it means you detect early, keep populations under threshold, and never let a problem reach the harvest. A mature program is mostly quiet, steady prevention, weekly scouting, sticky-trap trends, clean entry, light preventative foliar, with curative escalation as the rare exception rather than the routine.
points: Top mistakes: late scouting, spraying unidentified pests, treating after the window
Keep tools/PPE room-specific; keep biocontrol media out of irrigation
No fumigators in a flower facility; no metallic clamps in sprayers (heavy-metals risk)
Realistic goal: stay under threshold and protect the harvest, not zero pests
Mature IPM is mostly quiet prevention; curative spraying is the exception

figures.name: pitfalls_table
figures.desc: Two-column table: common mistake and the correct practice that prevents it

figures.name: prevention_vs_reaction
figures.desc: Bar comparison of effort/cost: prevention (scouting, hygiene, biocontrol) vs reactive cleanup after an outbreak

claims_needing_cite: Airborne fumigators can drift into buds and compromise a flowering crop
Metallic hose clamps in sprayers can rust and cause heavy-metals test failures
